summary:
The Great Famine of 1315–1322 was the worst famine in European history before the [[event:95|Black Death]], killing an estimated 10–25% of the population of northern Europe over seven years of crop failures, livestock disease, and social collapse, and severely weakening the resilience of European societies that would face the Black Death barely a generation later. The famine began with exceptionally cold and wet summers from 1315 onward — likely caused by a period of volcanic cooling and oceanic circulation changes — that ruined harvests across England, France, Germany, Scandinavia, and the Low Countries for multiple consecutive years.

The Great Famine exposed the fundamental vulnerability of medieval European agriculture: a predominantly subsistence system with minimal storage capacity and no effective mechanism for redistributing food across regions. When crops failed, populations starved; when animals died of murrain (livestock disease), the draft animals needed for plowing were lost, compounding the following year's food shortage. Contemporary chronicles describe scenes of mass starvation, abandoned children, parents eating their offspring, and the exhumation and eating of executed criminals — the breakdown of all normal social constraints under the pressure of absolute hunger. The famine's demographic and psychological impact — weakening populations, depleting agricultural labor, and traumatizing survivors — created conditions that made the subsequent [[event:95|Black Death's]] impact even more catastrophic than it might otherwise have been. The Great Famine marks the end of the medieval warm period's agricultural expansion and the beginning of the climate deterioration that characterized the late medieval 'Little Ice Age.'

read_more_links.title: Little Ice Age medieval climate deterioration
